title: Market structures
summary:
## The spectrum of competition

Market structures are classified by the **number of firms, barriers to entry, product differentiation and knowledge**. They range from **perfect competition** to **monopoly**, with **monopolistic competition** and **oligopoly** in between. Firms in all of them profit-maximise at **MC = MR**.

## Perfect competition

**Assumptions:** many small firms, identical (homogeneous) products, **no barriers to entry/exit**, perfect information, firms are **price takers**.
- Each firm faces a **horizontal AR = MR** (the market price).
- **Short run:** can make **supernormal profit** (or a loss) at MC = MR.
- **Long run:** entry/exit competes profit away → firms make only **normal profit** (AR = AC).
- **Efficiency:** achieves both **productive (min AC)** and **allocative (P = MC)** efficiency — the benchmark — but little dynamic efficiency (no supernormal profit to fund R&D).

## Monopoly

A **pure monopoly** is a single seller (in practice, ≥25% market share or ‘monopoly power’). **High barriers to entry** (economies of scale, legal patents, brand, control of resources) let it earn **long-run supernormal profit**.
- Downward-sloping AR with MR below it; profit-maximises at MC = MR, giving **higher price and lower output** than perfect competition.
- **Allocatively inefficient (P > MC)** and often **productively inefficient / X-inefficient**; can create **deadweight welfare loss**.
- **But:** economies of scale (a **natural monopoly**), supernormal profit funding **dynamic efficiency/innovation**, and lower prices from scale can be benefits.
- **Price discrimination** — charging different prices to different groups for the same good (needs market power, separable markets, different elasticities, no resale). Can raise producer surplus and output but reduce consumer surplus.

## Monopolistic competition

**Many firms, differentiated products, low barriers.** Each has slight price-setting power (downward AR).
- **Short run:** supernormal profit possible.
- **Long run:** entry erodes it → **normal profit** (AR tangent to AC), but at a point of **excess capacity** — neither productively nor allocatively efficient. Examples: restaurants, hairdressers.

## Oligopoly

A **few large firms dominate** (high **concentration ratio**); **interdependence** is the defining feature — each firm's action depends on rivals' reactions.
- **Kinked demand curve** model → **price rigidity** (rivals match price cuts but not rises), so firms compete on **non-price** terms (branding, quality, loyalty schemes).
- **Collusion:** firms may form a **cartel** to fix prices/output like a monopoly (illegal in most countries); **tacit collusion / price leadership** is subtler.
- **Game theory** (e.g. the **prisoner's dilemma**) shows why firms may not collude despite incentives — dominant strategies can lead to competition.

## Contestable markets

A market is **contestable** if entry and exit are **cheap and easy** (low sunk costs). The *threat* of entry (‘hit-and-run’) disciplines incumbents to keep prices and profits low even with few firms — so **behaviour** matters as much as structure.

## Why it matters

Market structures are the central A2 micro topic and the basis for competition policy (Unit 8). Compare structures on **efficiency, profit, price/output and consumer welfare** to power evaluation.

keyTerms.definition: Many price-taking firms with identical products and no barriers to entry; long-run normal profit.
A price taker accepts the market price; a price maker can influence price via a downward-sloping AR.
A single (or dominant) seller protected by high barriers to entry, earning long-run supernormal profit.
Obstacles (scale, patents, brand, sunk costs) that prevent new firms entering a market.
An industry where economies of scale mean one firm can supply the whole market at lowest cost.
Charging different prices to different consumers for the same good, needing market power and no resale.
Many firms with differentiated products and low barriers; long-run normal profit with excess capacity.
A market dominated by a few interdependent firms with high barriers to entry.
Firms agreeing to fix prices or output to act like a monopoly.
A market where low entry/exit costs mean the threat of entry disciplines incumbents.

diagrams: **Perfect competition:** horizontal AR=MR; short-run supernormal profit/loss vs long-run normal profit (AR=AC tangency).
**Monopoly:** downward AR, MR below it; MC=MR output; shade supernormal profit and the deadweight welfare loss vs the competitive (P=MC) output.
**Monopolistic competition long run:** AR tangent to AC at MC=MR output → normal profit with excess capacity.
**Oligopoly kinked demand curve:** demand kinked at the current price with a discontinuous MR → price rigidity.
**Price discrimination:** two sub-markets with different elasticities, each priced at its own MC=MR.
tips: Learn each structure by its **four assumptions** (number of firms, barriers, product, information) and its **long-run profit and efficiency** outcome.
Always draw the **deadweight welfare loss** on the monopoly diagram and compare with the competitive P = MC output.
Give a **balanced monopoly evaluation**: allocative/productive inefficiency vs economies of scale, natural monopoly and dynamic efficiency.
For oligopoly, use **interdependence + the kinked demand curve** to explain price rigidity, and **game theory/prisoner's dilemma** for collusion.
Bring in **contestability** to argue that *behaviour* (threat of entry) can matter more than the number of firms.
